content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kristopher J. Kokotaylo 
Assistant City Attorney 
City of San Leandro 
555 12th Street, Suite 1500 
Oakland, CA 94607 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-234 
Dear Mr. Kokotaylo: 
November 15, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice, on behalf of the City of San Leandro (the 
City) and San Leandro Planning Commissioner Tony Breslin (Commissioner Breslin), regarding 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the Act).1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Government Code Section I 090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. 
The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In 
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
Does Commissioner Breslin have a conflict of interest in participating in governmental 
decisions involving the proposed rezoning and development of existing commercial property to an 
apartment building due to the proximity of the proposed project site to the official's real property? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The decisions regarding the proposed rezoning and development will have a material 
financial effect on Commissioner Breslin's residence as discussed below. 
FACTS 
You are the City Attorney for the 15-square mile City of San Leandro in Alameda County. 
The City has a current population of 88,329. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are lo Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-234 
Page No. 2 
Commissioner Breslin and his wife currently own property on Cary Drive in San Leandro as 
their personal residence (the Property). The total fair market value of Commissioner Breslin's 
economic interest in the Property is more than $2,000. 
Mr. Tom Silva recently submitted an application to the San Leandro Community 
Development Department to rezone 13 88 Bancroft A venue and to develop a four story, 73-unit, 
Apartment Project. 
The Property's nearest point is 1,460 feet from the nearest point of 1388 Bancroft Avenue. 
Currently the following two businesses have active business licenses at 1380 Bancroft Avenue: 
(l) Bayview Medical Clinic, and (2) Aging Care Inc. Both appear to provide medical services. 
At 1300 Bancroft, there are four active businesses, including a foot spa, a massage establishment, a 
professional office, and a dentist's office. There are no active business licenses at 1388 Bancroft 
A venue. The Apartment Project would require demolishing the structure that houses those 
businesses. 
Community Development Department staff has deemed the application complete, and the 
matter is scheduled for a public hearing before the San Leandro Planning Commission. The 
Planning Commission will review the application, and forward a recommendation to the City 
Council. You state that staff will recommend to the Planning Commission that it should deny the 
rezone request and thus the approval of the Planned Development Project. A recommendation of 
denial does not trigger California Environmental Quality Act review. (See Pub. Res. Code, 
§§ 21000-21177; CEQA Guidelines Article 18, § 15270). If the Planning Commission rejects 
staffs recommendation to deny, then the project application will be remanded to staff for CEQA 
review, and findings for approval. 
Various City of San Leandro public officials and staff have already received numerous 
comments, both for and against, the project. Proponents assert the need for more housing in the 
City and the region in general. Opponents cite the project's size, magnitude, and allege various 
detrimental effects to the neighborhood if the project is approved and built, including traffic and 
parking impacts. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes, 
participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision which has a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. 
A public official must disqualify himself from the governmental decision at issue if the decision 
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his interest in real property, 
distinguishable from the public generally. 
Section 87103(b) provides that an official has a financial interest in "[a]ny real property in 
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest of at least $2,000." Section 87103 also 
provides that an official has a financial interest in his or her "personal finances, including those of 


FileNo. A-17-234 
Page No. 3 
his or her immediate family, also known as the 'personal financial effects' rule."2 We now consider 
the financial effect of the decisions at issue on the official's real property interests. 
Foreseeability: 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. (Section 87100.) The standard for 
foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. 
(Regulation 18701.) An interest is explicitly involved in a decision if the interest is a named party 
in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) 
Regulation 18701(b) provides: "[i]n general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." You 
stated that "Commissioner Breslin's financial interest in the Property is not explicitly involved in a 
decision on the proposed Apartment Project." The facts you provided also show that the official's 
residence is not explicitly involved in the decisions regarding the Apartment Project. 
Materiality: 
Regulation 18702.2(a) provides a list of circumstances under which the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in which an official has a 
financial interest is material. Based on your facts, the financial effect of the decision regarding the 
Project application on Commissioner Breslin's real property interest is material if the decisions: 
• Would change the income producing potential of the parcel of real property. (Regulation 
18702.2(a)(8).) 
You stated that the Apartment Project would be in the same neighborhood as the Property, 
Estudillo Estates. You further state: "[s]ince it will be an apartment building that will charge fair 
market value rents, it appears to me that there is a realistic possibility that if Commissioner Breslin 
were to ever rent the Property at fair market value, the approval of the Apartment Project would 
change the Property's rental income producing potential." 
• Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic 
levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real 
property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other 
factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official 
has a financial interest. (Regulation 18702.2(a)(l0).) 
You stated that "[i]t appears to be a realistic possibility that approval would increase 
population density and thereby increase noise and decrease privacy in the Estudillo Estates 
neighborhood if appropriate mitigations are not imposed. It also appears to be a realistic possibility 
that the apartment building project would impact parking in the neighborhood through residents and 
2 Effects on an official's personal finances are not considered separately from the effect on an official's interest 
in real property. (Regulation 18702.S(c).) Thus, we do not consider the personal financial effects rule further. 


File No. A-17-234 
Page No. 4 
guests of tenants parking in the surrounding neighborhoods if appropriate mitigations are not 
imposed. Finally, construction of the apartment building project would presumably affect noise 
levels, air quality, and odors within the neighborhood." 
• Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the 
circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its 
reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property. 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(l 2).) 
You stated as follows: "Under this 'catch-all' provision it appears to be a realistic possibility 
that once the apartment building project comes to the Planning Commission, given its magnitude 
within the neighborhood, it is reasonably foreseeable that it would influence the market value of the 
Property." 
Based on the facts you provided, the decision regarding the Project application on 
Commissioner Breslin's real property interest is material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(8), (a)(IO) 
and (a}(l2.)3 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
JMF:jgl 
Brian Lau 
Assistant·Generat 
By:/!,.. n M. Feser 
/
Senior Counsel, Legal Division 
3 In your request for advice you have also referenced the public generally exception, which allows an official 
to take part in a decision if the effect on the official's interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally. (See Regulation 18703.) However, there is no indication from the facts provided that the public generally 
exception applies to the decisions at hand.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Commissioner Breslin have a conflict of interest in participating in governmental 
decisions involving the proposed rezoning and development of existing commercial property to an 
apartment building due to the proximity of the proposed project site to the official's real property?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The decisions regarding the proposed rezoning and development will have a material 
financial effect on Commissioner Breslin's residence as discussed below.
